3.why: The cursor must know where the string ends so it never reads past it.
3.invariant: n is fixed for the whole scan.

4.why: Parsing always starts from the left.
4.invariant: pos is the next character to inspect.

5.happening: Character {pos} is a space — skip it.
5.why: Leading whitespace is ignored before anything else is read.

5.happening: The cursor is off the spaces now (or the string ended).
5.why: The first non-space character is where the real parsing begins.
5.invariant: pos points at the first non-space character.

6.happening: Step the cursor past the space to index {pos + 1}.
6.why: Keep skipping until a non-space appears.
6.invariant: Everything skipped so far was whitespace.

7.happening: Mark index {pos} as where the content starts.
7.why: Everything to the left of here was leading whitespace, now shown dimmed.

8.why: If no sign appears, the default is positive.
8.invariant: sign is +1 unless a minus is found.

9.why: -1 marks 'no sign', so the visual highlights a sign only if one exists.
9.invariant: sign_idx stays -1 unless a +/- is read.

10.happening: The first content character is a minus sign.
10.why: A minus makes the whole result negative.
10.invariant: At most one sign character is consumed.

10.happening: The first content character is not a minus.
10.why: We still need to check for a plus before reading digits.
10.invariant: At most one sign character is consumed.

11.why: The digits that follow will be negated at the end.

12.happening: Remember index {pos} as the sign character.
12.why: So the visual can mark it distinctly from the digits.
12.invariant: sign_idx points at the one sign character.

13.happening: Step the cursor past the sign to index {pos + 1}.
13.why: The sign is consumed; digits come next.
13.invariant: The cursor never re-reads the sign.

14.happening: The first content character is a plus sign.
14.why: A plus is allowed but leaves the number positive.
14.invariant: At most one sign character is consumed.

14.happening: No sign character at all — the number is positive by default.
14.why: Parsing jumps straight to reading digits.

15.happening: Remember index {pos} as the sign character.
15.why: So the visual can mark it distinctly from the digits.
15.invariant: sign_idx points at the one sign character.

16.happening: Step the cursor past the plus to index {pos + 1}.
16.why: The sign is consumed; digits come next.
16.invariant: The cursor never re-reads the sign.

17.why: Digits will be folded into it one at a time.
17.invariant: res holds the magnitude read so far, before the sign is applied.

18.why: Any result above this must be clamped down to it.

19.why: Any negative result below this must be clamped up to it.

20.happening: Character {pos} ('{s[pos]}') is a digit — read it.
20.why: Digits are consumed until a non-digit or the end of the string.
20.invariant: Reading stops at the first non-digit.

20.happening: The cursor is on a non-digit (or the string ended) — stop reading.
20.why: atoi consumes digits only up to the first non-digit character.
20.invariant: res now holds the full magnitude.

21.happening: Convert the digit character '{s[pos]}' to its numeric value.
21.why: Character codes for '0'..'9' are consecutive, so subtracting '0' gives the value.

22.happening: res ({res}) would overflow if we fold in {digit} — clamp instead.
22.why: We check BEFORE multiplying, because res * 10 + digit could exceed the 32-bit range and wrap.
22.invariant: Overflow is caught before it can happen.

22.happening: Folding in {digit} keeps res ({res}) safely in range.
22.why: res * 10 + {digit} will still fit in 32 bits.

23.happening: The number is positive — clamp to the maximum.
23.why: A positive overflow is pinned at 2147483647.
23.invariant: The clamped answer is the nearest valid bound.

23.happening: The number is negative — clamp to the minimum instead.
23.why: A negative overflow is pinned at -2147483648.
23.invariant: The clamped answer is the nearest valid bound.

24.why: The positive value overflowed the 32-bit range.

26.why: The negative value overflowed the 32-bit range.

27.why: Once clamped, more digits cannot change the answer.

28.why: Shifting left one place and adding the digit builds the number left to right.
28.invariant: res holds every digit read so far, in order.

29.happening: Step the cursor to the next character, index {pos + 1}.
29.why: Move on to read the following digit, if any.

30.why: The digits were read as a positive magnitude; the sign is applied at the end.
30.invariant: The final value carries the correct sign.

31.why: The string has been fully converted, signed, and range-checked.

init.happening: Reading the string length and setting up the sign and total.
init.why: We need bounds and defaults before scanning.

loop.happening: Skipping spaces, or reading digits one at a time.
loop.why: The two loops handle leading whitespace and then the number.
loop.invariant: The cursor only ever moves forward.

move.happening: Advancing the cursor past a character.
move.why: Each character is inspected exactly once.

update.happening: Recording the sign or folding a digit into the running total.
update.why: res and sign together build the final value.
update.invariant: res holds the magnitude read so far.

check.happening: Testing for a sign character or for a potential overflow.
check.why: The branches decide the sign and whether to clamp.
check.invariant: No character is consumed on a pure check line.

return.happening: Returning the parsed, signed, range-checked integer.
return.why: Either the digits ran out or the value was clamped.
